utterances: Any other examples of the kinds of work that veterans did that - they haven't been able to use those skills when they get into the civilian workforce?
Sure.

I mean, it really does run the spectrum from some professional careers, such as teachers, legal aides, and even lawyers and nurses - all of those are military professions that translate readily to the civilian job market, but that require specific certification - some at the federal level, some at the state level.
What about soldiers who have physical disabilities, or even those who have experience mental trauma as a result of the stress of the war?
Again, another significant challenge.
To give that question some context, about 2.8 million men and women have served since 2001.
And the rate at which they're leaving military service with enduring physical or psychological disabilities, it's actually unprecedented in U.S.
history.
About 30 percent, conservatively, will live out the rest of their lives with an enduring physical or psychological disability.
For many of them, what we describe as traditional employment - the 9-to-5 job - may be a challenge.
One of the interesting things that we're seeing in this generation of vets is many with disabilities are turning to self-employment.

For example, you know, there's a young Marine that I've been working with now for several years who is a successful business owner today, but had spent almost two years in the hospital recovering from his injuries.
And, you know, he would tell me that almost every single day, everything that he had to do, he required someone to help him with.
And when he talks about business ownership, he couches it in this idea that, I have been given back autonomy and control over life.
And I think that motivation is probably present in a lot of the veterans that we see pursuing self-employment.
I can see where self-employment is a good alternative for some vets.
But is it really the solution to this unemployment problem we've been talking about?
I do not believe it is.
You know, I think it's an element of the solution.
One of the other things we need to get right for this generation of veterans is education.
The post-9/11 GI bill has created unprecedented opportunities for higher education.
But we're seeing veterans who pursue higher education dropping out of colleges at a very high rate.
Anywhere between 50 - and some folks will say almost 80 percent of those that start a four-year degree on the post-9/11 GI bill, are actually quitting school.
And that contributes to the unemployment challenges that we're seeing.
Michael Haynie is executive director of the Institute For Veterans and Military Families.
Michael, thanks so much for joining us.
Thanks for having me.
It's MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
Good morning.
I'm Steve Inskeep.
And I'm Renee Montagne.
A U.S.
commander is defending Pakistan's effort to fight insurgent groups.
General David Petraeus is in charge of American troops throughout the Middle East and South Asia.
General Petraeus sat down to talk after President Obama ordered 30,000 more troops to Afghanistan.
And we had questions about the country where the president cannot send more forces.
General Petraeus believes that his troops will face insurgents who hide in Pakistan, just across the long and twisting border.

These are very remote areas, high altitude - you know, eight to 12,000 feet - in which these elements have operated all the way back to the decades in which we funded many of them when they were the Mujahedeen who were fighting the Soviet occupation of Afghanistan.
That is a conflict from decades ago, and Petraeus spoke of specific insurgents who've spent much of that time in these wild lands.
Consider a man named Siraj Haqqani.
His father fought the Russians, and now the son is blamed for kidnappings and really vicious attacks on Kabul.
If you stuck a pin on a map to mark the place General Petraeus thinks the son is, you would stick the pin in Pakistan.
It's immoral and it's irresponsible, but it's not illegal.
Evidently not.
But you know, I'm sure it made their job much easier to ignore that one little piece of paper called a search warrant.
And that's one aspect of this story that makes it pretty interesting.
He was doing a pretty good job.
He was cleaning up a lot of people that, well, a lot of people wanted cleaned up.
I disagree with that.
I don't think that there is a huge methamphetamine problem in Gerald.
There is certainly a meth problem, as there is everywhere.
The amount of drugs they've confiscated is miniscule compared to the damage they've done.
I think a significant amount of marijuana is the biggest recovery they made.
But the police seemed to go along with him because they thought he was getting results.
The police, I believe, went along with him because they were able to write impressive press releases.
Huh.
And his methods were less than gentle, as I understand it.
That's absolutely for sure.
He kicked the doors literally off the hinges in at least two homes, but always kicked his way in.

Immediately ordered everybody to the floor, always had a gun that was described as a sawed-off shotgun with a pistol grip, and in many cases, pointed it to the heads or faces of the people who he was arresting.
Did - often did this with their children in the room, watching it all.
Handcuffed everyone, the moms, the dads.
Anybody that was in the house was handcuffed, drug out into the front yard in front of their neighbors, and then, were asked to start naming names.
And then asked to start naming names.
And you're suggesting that - who was - you know, when you found out who he was, who was he really?
He was an unemployed past security guard who had worked part time, at best, lived with his in-laws, I believe.
He and his wife lived with her parents and was no one connected with law enforcement.
And do we know why he did this?
No, we don't.
He's told the media since that time that he did it to try to help.
His story is that he came to town on an innocent conversation with the chief of police and was kind of lured into this kicking-down-doors-and-being-a-federal-agent scam.
The chief of police said at that time that he came in claiming to be a federal agent.
And that he...
I'm not sure which story is correct.
But he also claimed that Sergeant Bill had set up this phony phone line to check his credentials.
That's correct.
The chief of police did say that, and that when he faxed the information in, he called to confirm, a female answered, said that everything was in order.
And then Mr. Jakob told him a few days later his boss had approved his transfer.
He now belonged to the Gerald Police Department.
And where...
He was there at their disposal.
And there at their disposal and thus began - and how long did this go on?
It went on for at least a month.
It went on, on a really regular basis.
They said he had come in to town about two months prior to that and would just come in a couple of times a week.
Now, as I understand it, though you report for the Gasconade County Republican and that's based in a town, what, about 10 miles away from Gerald, that you live in Gerald.
That's correct.
And the Gasconade County Republican has a section in their paper that covers the town of Gerald.
And I wonder, what's been the reaction of your neighbors to disclosures in your newspaper?
The people that I've talked to are angry.
They're very, very angry that this happened, that his credentials were never checked, that checks weren't made on him that are made on peoples' dogs.
If you have a dog in the city of Gerald, you're required to show paperwork that it's been vaccinated and buy a license for it.
And even that cursory check wasn't done on a man that was allowed to carry a firearm and have access to all of the records in city hall.
Hm.
Let's see if we can get a caller on the line.
And if you'd like to join us, our phone number is 800-989-8255.
Email is talk@npr.org.
Our guest is Linda Trest, a staff writer for the Gasconade County Republican.
And why don't we go to Michael, and Michael's on the line with us from Boston.
Hi.

What does it say about police enforcement of the law in general, and the drugs war in particular, that we're appalled by this private citizen doing many of the things that actual federal agents do every day and doesn't seem to raise an eyebrow?
They do get warrants, but quite often, the warrants are based upon, let's say, less-than-solid evidence.
But beyond that, his behavior seemed quite normal and we're appalled, whereas, you know, if he had a real badge then it would be OK?
Well, not the warrant part, but Linda Trest, I wonder if you've thought about that.
I have thought about that and I'm glad he brought that question up.
The main focus of the story, since it's become national, has been on, you know, what a Hooterville (ph) this makes our town look like.
It makes small-town America look silly, and I don't think that's the case.
I think that's a very valid point.
The actions of Mr. Jakob and the Gerald Police Department are not uncommon.
Too often we look the other way when one small portion of anyone's civil rights are violated, and as a nation, we're all made smaller by that.

And I think it's time that we insisted that all law enforcement agencies, federal, local, everywhere, re-read the Constitution and really understand what those are, because that Constitution covers everyone, even low-life drug users.
They still have civil rights and they need to be upheld.
Michael, thanks for the call.
Let's see if we can go now to - this is Jim.
Jim is with us from Eagle River in Alaska.
Did you ever eat there?
Hello, thank you.
Yes, I'd like to comment that the police chief in Gerald reportedly claims he tried a few checks and he thought it would - he claims essentially incompetence.
And I've noticed a lot of people who have taken oath claim incompetence when they're discovered cheating.
And it appears to me incompetence is frequently a cover for corruption and should be treated as an admission of corruption when someone claims incompetence.
Well, what's happened to the police chief there in Gerald?
